trajectory_summary: Bucky completed the verify section, captured the bill, and booked a Thursday 2PM appointment — the functional outcome is real. But he left significant emotional leverage on the table throughout: Karen's widowhood disclosure went unacknowledged, her 'free solar in your ZIP code' misconception was never corrected, and her debt-free identity statement was met with silence before a scheduling pivot. The appointment is booked but structurally fragile — the closer inherits a customer whose core financial constraint was never bridged.
outcome_dispute: The call produced a booked appointment, so a 'booked' tag would be accurate on its face. However, the appointment carries conditional fragility: Karen's explicit debt-free constraint and her near-zero out-of-pocket expectation were not addressed. If the v1 tag is 'booked/clean,' that overstates the quality. A more accurate tag would be 'booked/conditional' — the sit is at risk if the closer's program options don't align with Karen's stated financial identity.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Conditional booking — values-level constraint left unacknowledged
primary_coaching_focus.quote: But I don't want anything that I'm gonna have to owe over time. I'm debt free. I don't wanna owe anything.
primary_coaching_focus.why: On this call, Bucky treated Karen's debt-free identity statement as background noise and pivoted to scheduling without a word of acknowledgment or a bridge to the specialist. The closer now inherits a customer whose stated financial identity may conflict with every standard solar financing structure Ion offers. This is the single highest-risk gap in the call — not because Bucky should have resolved it (he shouldn't; that's closer-tier), but because he needed to acknowledge it, bridge it, and flag it. The manager should use this moment to drill the three-part move: acknowledge the values statement → bridge to specialist → flag in handoff brief.

key_moments.quote: I'm a widow now, and the bills are crazy when you live alone and you only have one income coming in.
key_moments.stewart_read: Karen volunteers widowhood and single-income status in the same breath as her bill pain. This is a trust gift — she's telling Bucky exactly why solar matters to her life, not just her wallet. Bucky's response is to move directly into verify questions. The schema is explicit: vulnerability disclosure is not an interruption to procedure; it's the emotional anchor the entire pitch should build from. Reactive read holds — there is no strategic reason to skip past this.

key_moments.quote: I'm hearing all this stuff that, you know, if you live in the ZIP code, it's free, and I don't know how true that is.
key_moments.stewart_read: Karen is flagging a specific misconception — 'free solar by ZIP code' — that she herself is skeptical of ('I don't know how true that is'). This is a scam-anxiety signal dressed as a question. Bucky does not address it at all; he moves to verify. The risk: Karen arrives at the closer appointment still carrying this misconception, and if the closer's program doesn't match her 'free' expectation, the sit collapses. An honest reframe here ('That's a common thing people hear — here's what the program actually looks like') would have set accurate expectations and built trust simultaneously.

Bucky asks who else is on the title and Karen discloses the property is held in a trust. Bucky follows up to confirm the trust is in her name ('But trust is in your name?') and Karen confirms. Bucky moves on. The verify item is technically checked, but the trust structure has potential contract-execution implications — depending on Ion's legal requirements, a trust-held property may require additional documentation or a trustee signature at close. This is not setter-tier content to resolve, but it is handoff-brief content the closer needs flagged.

key_moments.quote: I was outside if that makes more sense. Okay. I'm picking up dead sticks that had fallen from the winter, and I have and I was just yakking with my neighbor who, god bless him, burns the dead wood for me.
key_moments.stewart_read: Karen is describing active yard maintenance — clearing winter debris, working with a neighbor on property upkeep. This is a property-investment signal: she cares about her home, she's physically active in maintaining it, and she has a neighbor relationship that suggests community rootedness. Combined with her 2020 roof and her explicit statement that she plans to stay ('I figured, well, I could check'), this paints a long-tenure homeowner who is invested in the property. Bucky laughs along but never reflects the signal back — no 'sounds like you take good care of the place' or any anchor that ties her property pride to the value of a long-term solar investment.
